content.text:
The even permutations form a distinguished subgroup of $S_n$ called the 
**alternating group**. This group plays a central role in the 
theory of solvable groups and the impossibility of solving the general 
quintic equation by radicals.

content.title: Alternating Group
content.content:
The **alternating group** $A_n$ is the 
set of all even permutations in $S_n$:
\[
 A_n = \{\sigma \in S_n : \text{sgn}(\sigma) = 1\}
\]

$A_n$ is nonempty (contains $\iota$). By the sign homomorphism:

- If $\sigma, \tau \in A_n$, then $\text{sgn}(\sigma\tau) = 
 \text{sgn}(\sigma)\text{sgn}(\tau) = 1$, so $\sigma\tau \in A_n$.

- If $\sigma \in A_n$, then $\text{sgn}(\sigma^{-1}) = 
 \text{sgn}(\sigma) = 1$, so $\sigma^{-1} \in A_n$.

For the order: the map $\sigma \mapsto (1\ 2)\sigma$ is a bijection from 
$A_n$ to the set of odd permutations (since sgn$((1\ 2)\sigma) = -1$). 
Thus even and odd permutations are equinumerous, and 
$|A_n| = |S_n|/2 = n!/2$.

content.title: $A_n$ is Generated by $3$-Cycles
content.content:
For $n \geq 3$, the alternating group $A_n$ is generated by the set of 
all $3$-cycles.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Every element of $A_n$ is a product of an even number of transpositions. 
We show that any product of two transpositions can be written as a 
product of $3$-cycles.

Case 1: $(a\ b)(a\ b) = \iota$ (the identity, an empty product of 
$3$-cycles).

Case 2: $(a\ b)(a\ c) = (a\ c\ b)$ (a single $3$-cycle).

Case 3: $(a\ b)(c\ d) = (a\ c\ b)(a\ c\ d)$ (product of two $3$-cycles).

content.title: Small alternating groups
content.problem:
- $A_1 = \{\iota\}$, order $1$.

- $A_2 = \{\iota\}$, order $1$.

- $A_3 = \{\iota, (1\ 2\ 3), (1\ 3\ 2)\} \cong \Z_3$, order $3$.

- $A_4$: order $12$. Contains $\iota$, eight $3$-cycles, and three 
 products of disjoint transpositions.

- $A_5$: order $60$. This is the smallest non-abelian simple group.

content.title: $A_5$ is Simple
content.content:
The alternating group $A_5$ has no normal subgroups other than $\{e\}$ 
and $A_5$ itself.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This fact is crucial for proving that the general quintic polynomial 
cannot be solved by radicals (Galois theory).

items: List all elements of $A_3$ and verify it is cyclic.

Show that $A_4$ has no subgroup of order $6$, even though $6$ 
 divides $|A_4| = 12$.

The group $V = \{\iota, (1\ 2)(3\ 4), (1\ 3)(2\ 4), (1\ 4)(2\ 3)\}$ 
 is a subgroup of $A_4$. Verify this and identify it as the Klein 
 four-group.

marginNotes.text:
Then you will understand what is right 
and just and fair—every good path.
